title: Welcome!
text:
Nice to meet you! I’m a passionate statistician looking for opportunities to make the world a better and safer place using data science and statistical methods. My goals are to apply statistics to real world problems in the area of cybersecurity.


Computational statistics, machine learning, data processing, data visualization, anomaly detection and adversarial risk analysis.

title: about
text:
I’m Nate Byford,
A graduate student at Baylor University in the department of Statistical Sciences. I obtained my undergraduate B.S. in statistics at Baylor University as well in 2022.
When I’m not in my office taking care of business, whether that be homework, studying, teaching, or consulting work. I can be found outside mountain biking or hiking in nearby Cameron Park. I also play the guitar and bass and can be found gigging on occasion around town or on campus.
My primary interests are utilizing statistical methods to solve real world problems and make the world a safer place. Since I’ve always been interested in computers and other technology type of things I’m particularly interested in finding applications for statistics in cyber security. I also enjoy data visualization and data modeling, since I can do all that on my computer and compare different plots or models to get the best fit for the problem at hand.

title: Past Projects

MoWaTER

Analysis of Water filter efficiency and cleanliness at Denver water’s foothill water treatment plant. As a part of MoWaTER summer research group @ Baylor Univesity 2020.

title: Past Projects
text:
Anomaly Detection

As a part of STA 5360 at Baylor university my final project was comparing different approaches to anomaly detection. These methods included a simple yet good linear model method, seasonal time series decomposition using loess (STL), isolation forests, and a neural network.


Benford’s Law

Analysis of statistical tests that can be used to test for Benford’s Law. As a part of RUSIS @ OSU 2021.

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text: All notes come from reading Casella and Berger, and slides from STA 5381 and STA 5382.

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text:
Set Theory
If probability is the foundation of statistics, then set theory is the cornerstone of probability. It is important to have a basic understanding of set theory to understand probability and statistics.
Sample Space: The set \(\mathcal{S}\) of all possible outcomes of an experiment is called the sample space for the experiment. Other possible notations of the sample space are \(\{\Omega \text{ or } \mathcal{X}\}\).
Since the sample space contains all possible values of a given experiment we need to recognize the two types of sample spaces: countable and uncountable. A countable sample space is one that we can count all the possible outcomes either values or specific outcomes, these tend to have specific set of possible outcomes or an integer value. Uncountable sample spaces have infinitely many possible outcomes, an example of this would be the sample space \(\mathcal{S} \in (0, \infty)\).
Event: Any collection of possible outcomes, that is any partition of the set \(\mathcal{S}\). This includes the total partition of the entire set \(\mathcal{S} \in \mathcal{S}\).
There are two types of events, simple events and complex events.

Simple events: An event with a single outcome, a simple event can only happen one way.
Complex events: The combination of two or more simple events. The number if ways a complex event can occur is determined by counting techniques.

Set Operations Let \(A\) be a set from the sample space \(\mathcal{S}\) and \(B\) be another set from the sample space \(\mathcal{S}\).

Union: \[A \cup B = \left\{\omega \in \mathcal{S}: \omega \in A ~{} \mathbf{or} ~{} \omega \in B\right\}\]
Intersection: \[A \cap B = \left\{\omega \in \mathcal{S}: \omega \in A ~{} \mathbf{and} ~{} \omega \in B\right\}\]
Compliment: \[A^{c} = A^{\prime} = \left\{\omega \in \mathbf{S}: \omega \notin A\right\}\]

When two sets share nothing between the two, then \(A \cap B = \emptyset\). Where \(\emptyset\) is called the null or empty set, the set that contains nothing.

Properties of set operators

Commutativity: \[A \cup B = B \cup A\] \[A \cap B = B \cap A\]
Associativity: \[A \cup (B \cup C) = (A \cup B) \cup C\] \[A \cap (B \cap C) = (A \cap B) \cap C\]
Distributive: \[A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)\] \[A \cup (B \cap C) = (A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C)\]
DeMorgan’s Law: \[(A \cup B)^{c} = A^{c} \cap B^{c}\] \[(A \cap B)^{c} = A^{c} \cup B^{c}\] Note: Using a Venn diagram to show these properties does not count as mathematical proof and a proof can be found using the subsets and showing they are equal from both sides.

Both unions and intersections can be expanded to infinite sets. \[\cup_{i = 1}^{\infty} A_i = \left\{\omega \in \mathcal{S}: \omega \in A_i \text{ For some } i\right\}\] \[\cap_{i = 1}^{\infty} A_i = \left\{\omega \in \mathcal{S}: \omega \in A_i \text{ For all } i\right\}\] Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are said to be disjoint if \(A \cap B = \emptyset\). The events \(A_1, A_2, \ldots\) are said to be pairwise disjoint if \(A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset\) for all \(i \ne j\).
Disjoint sets are sets with no points in common.
If \(A_1, A_2, \ldots\) are pairwise disjoint and \(\cup_{i=1}^{\infty}A_i = S\), then the collection \(A_1, A_2, \ldots\) is a partition of \(S\).

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text:
Basics of probability theory

“The theory of probability is founded on the assumption that it is possible to associate a numerical measure with the likelihood of each event.” - Andrey Kolmogorov

When an experiment is performed, the result is an outcome \(\omega\) in the sample space \(\mathcal{S}\). The probability of any outcome \(\omega \in \mathcal{S}\) is the limit of the relative frequency of the event, \(\omega\), as the number of times the experiment is performed goes to infinity.

Sigma algebras (\(\sigma\)-algebra)
A sigma algebra or Borel field is the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. The requirements for a \(\sigma\)-algebra/Borel field(\(\mathcal{B}\)) are

\(\emptyset \in \mathcal{B}\) (The null set is contained within the \(\sigma\)-algebra)
If \(A \in \mathcal{B}\), then \(A^{c} \in \mathcal{B}\) also. (Closed under complementation)
If \(A_1, A_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{B}\), then \(\cup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i \in \mathcal{B}\) (Closed under countable unions)

The smallest possible \(\sigma\)-algebra is called the trivial set and contains the sample space \(\mathcal{S}\) and the null set \(\emptyset\).
We tend not to be interested in the trivial set, we are more interested in the smallest set that isn’t the trivial set. The power set(\(\mathcal{P_S}\)) is the next smallest \(\sigma\)-algebra, this is the set of all the subsets within \(\mathcal{S}\) and \(\emptyset\). The cardinality or size of the power set is: \[\mathcal{C(P_S)} = 2^{\mathcal{C(S)}}\]


Axioms of probability
Given a sample space \(\mathcal{S}\) and an associated \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal{B}\), a probability function is a function \(P\) with domain \(\mathcal{B}\) that satisfies:

\(P(A) \ge 0, ~{} \text{ for all } ~{} A \in {\mathcal{B}}\)
\(P(S) = 1\)
If \(A_1, A_2, ...\) are pairwise disjoint, then \(P \left(\cup_{i=1}^\infty A_i \right) = \Sigma_{i=1}^\infty P(A_i)\)

These are the axioms of probability or Kolmogorov’s axioms.
There are some people who disagree with the third axiom, the axiom of countable additivity. Specifically a school of statistics led by deFinetti, who has decided to replace it with the axiom of finite additivity.
That is, if \(A \in \mathcal{B}\) and \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) are disjoint, then \[P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\] Although the axiom of finite additivity is easier to prove or see, it brings with it some unexpected consequences that the axiom of countable additivity does not introduce. Therefore, it is better to use the first set of axioms (Kolmogorov’s axioms).
Probability space
A probability space can be represented by the probability triple denoted as \((\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{B}, P)\), where \(\mathcal{S}\) is the sample space, \(\mathcal{B}\) is a Borel field of \(\mathcal{S}\), and \(P\) is a valid probability statement.
Let \(\mathcal{S} = \{ \omega_1, \ldots, \omega_n \}\) be a finite set and \(\mathcal{B}\) be any \(\sigma\)-algebra of \(\mathcal{S}\). Let \(p_1, \ldots, p_n\) be non-negative real numbers that sum to 1. For any \(A \in \mathcal{B}\) define \(P(A)\) by \[P(A) = \sum_{\{i: s_i \in A\}} p_i\]


Calculus of Probabilities
If \(P\) is a valid probability function and \(A\) is any set in \(\mathcal{B}\)

\(P(\emptyset) = 0\), where \(\emptyset\) is the null set;
\(P(A) \le 1\);
\(P(A^{c}) = 1 - P(A)\).

Probability of sets and set operations \[\begin{align*}
  P(A \cup B) &= P(A) + P(B) \\
    P(A \cap B^c) &= P(A) - P(A \cap B) \\
    P(A \cup B) &= P(A) +P(B) - P(A \cap B) \\
    \text{If } A &\subset B \text{ then } P(A) \le P(B)
\end{align*}\] Bonferoni’s inequality \[P(A \cap B) \ge P(A) + P(B) - 1\]
Law of total probability \[P(A) = \Sigma_{i = 1}^\infty P(A \cap C_i)\] For \(C_1, C_2, ... \in {\mathcal{B}}\), where \(C_1, C_2, ...\) are a partition of \(\mathcal{S}\). This will come in use a bit later when we use Bayes theorem and conditionals.
Boole’s Inequality \[ P(\cup_{i = 1}^\infty A_i) \le \Sigma P(A_i)\] For any collection of sets, \(A_1, A_2, \ldots \in {\mathcal{B}}\)

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text: Counting Techniques

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text:
Counting Techniques and Enumerating Outcomes
Fundamental Theorem of Counting: If a job consists of \(k\) separate tasks, the \(i\)-th of which can be done in \(n_i\) ways. \(i = 1, 2, ..., k\), then the entire job can be done in \(n_1 \times n_2 \times ... \times n_k\) ways.

What is a “job”? A “job” is what produces of the final set, examples include tossing a coin \(n\) times, tossing two dice one at a time. Basically the “job” is the experiment.
What is a “task”? A “task” is the action being done over and over for the job, examples include, flipping a coin, rolling a die, etc.

There are two properties of the experiment that must be known to determine the size of the sample space.

Sampling Scheme: with or without replacement
Outcomes: ordered or unordered.

Sampling Schemes

without replacement

Once a unit is selected for the sample it is removed from the population, can’t be sampled again.
Cannot have repeat units in the outcome.

With replacement

When a unit is selected for the sample it is placed back into the population, can be sampled again.
Can have repeat units in the outcome.


Outcome

Ordered outcomes

When an experiment involves two or more tasks, if the same results have a different ordering it is a different outcome.

Unordered outcomes

When an experiment involves two or more tasks, if the same results have a different ordering it is the same outcome, does not create a new outcome.



Counting Techniques
Ordered without Replacement
If a population with \(n\) units has \(r\) units selected without replacement. If the outcomes are ordered, the the number of outcomes is \[\begin{equation*}
  \mathcal{C(S)} = \frac{n!}{(n - r)!}
\end{equation*}\]
Ordered with Replacement
If a population with \(n\) units has \(r\) units selected with replacement. If the outcomes are ordered, the the number of outcomes is \[\begin{equation*}
  \mathcal{C(S)} = n^r
\end{equation*}\]
Unordered without Replacement
If a population with \(n\) units has \(r\) units selected without replacement. If the outcomes are unordered, the the number of outcomes is \[\begin{equation*}
  \mathcal{C(S)} = \binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{(n - r)! r!}
\end{equation*}\] This notation is called choose, here it would be said “There are \(n\) choose \(r\) possible outcomes.”
Unordered with Replacement
If a population with \(n\) units has \(r\) units selected with replacement. If the outcomes are unordered, the the number of outcomes is \[\begin{equation*}
  \mathcal{C(S)} = \binom{n + r - 1}{r} = \frac{(n + r - 1)!}{(n - 1)! r!}
\end{equation*}\]


Enumerating outcomes
To apply the counting techniques to compute probabilities enumerate the outcomes and then divide by the cardinality of the sample space. \[ P(A) = \frac{{\cal{C}}(A)}{{\cal{C}(S)}}  = \frac{{\cal{C}}(A)}{N} \] Ways to enumerate the outcomes:

Count up the amount of possibilities (Simple examples only).
Use counting techniques to determine \(\mathcal{C}(A)\).

In some cases the sample may need to be split into more than one event to use counting techniques. If so we would use the Fundamental Theorem of Counting to get the total number of outcomes.

title: Theory of Statistics notes
section: Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, and Distribution Functions
text:
Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, and Distribution Functions
Consider the sample space \(\cal{S}\) and \(\cal{B}\) a \(\sigma\)-algebra of the sets in \(\cal{S}\). Let \(A\) and \(B\) be any two events in \(\cal{B}\) with \(P(B) > 0\). Then the conditional probability of \(A\) given \(B\) (has occurred), is written as \(P(A|B)\) is \[ P(A|B) = \frac{P(AB)}{P(B)} \] Bayes Rule
From the expression for conditional probability we can derive the following. \[\begin{align*}
  P(A|B) &= \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \\
  \Rightarrow P(A \cap B) &= P(A|B)P(B) \\
  \Rightarrow P(B \cap A) &= P(B|A)P(A) \\
  \Rightarrow P(A|B) &= \frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}
\end{align*}\] Consider a sample space \(\cal{S}\) and a Borel field \(\cal{B}\) of the sets in \(\cal{S}\). Let \(A_1, A_2, ... \in \cal{B}\) be a partition of \(\cal{S}\) and \(B \in \cal{B}\) another set with \(P(B) > 0\). Then for each \(i = 1, 2, ...\) \[P(A_i|B) = \frac{P(B|A_i)P(A_i)}{\Sigma_{j=1}^\infty P(B|A_j)P(A_j)}\]
The denominator comes from the law of total probability.
Possibly the single most important property in statistics, independence.
Let \(\cal{S}\) be a sample space, and \(\cal{B}\) a Borel field of the sets in \(\cal{S}\). Let \(A\) and \(B\) be any two events in \(\cal{B}\) such that \(P(A)>0\) and \(P(B)>0\). The events \(A\) and \(B\) are said to be statistically independent (or independent) if \[\begin{align*}
  P(AB) &= P(A)P(B), \text{ or} \\
  P(A|B) = P(A) &\text{ or } P(B|A) = P(B)
\end{align*}\] If \(A\) and \(B\) are not independent, then they are dependent events. Another way to think of independence in terms of conditional probability is: if knowing that one event has occurred does not change the probability that the other event will occur.
Mutually Exclusive Events
Let \(\mathcal{S}\) be a sample space, and \(\mathcal{B}\) a \(\sigma\)-algebra on the sets in \(\cal{S}\). Let \(A\) and \(B\) be any two mutually exclusive events in \(\mathcal{B}\) with \(P(A)>0\) and \(P(B)>0\). Then \(A\) and \(B\) are statistically dependent events.
Independence of Complements
If \(A\) and \(B\) are independent events, then the following pairs are also independent.

\(A\) and \(B^c\)
\(A^c\) and \(B\)
\(A^c\) and \(B^c\)

Independence of Multiple Events
Also called : A collection of events \(A_1, A_2, ... , A_n\) are mutually independent if for any sub collection \(A_{i1}, ..., A_{ik}\) we have \[ P\left(\bigcap_{j=1}^k A_{ij}\right) = \prod_{j=1}^k P(A_{ij}) \]
Random Variables
Most experiments are easily described using a summary variable instead of the original probability structure.
A random variable is a function from the sample space \(\cal{S}\) into the real numbers.

The domain of \(A\) is \(\cal{S}\). Therefore, for each \(\omega_i \in \cal{S}\), we have \(X(\omega_i) = x_i\), where \(x_i \in\) real numbers.
The range of \(X(\omega)\) is all real numbers, or a subset of all real numbers.

Induced Probability Function
Consider a finite sample space \(\cal{S} = \{\omega_1, \omega_2, ... , \omega_n\}\) with probability function \(P\). Define the random variable \(X({\cal{S}}) \rightarrow {\cal{X}}\) where \({\cal{X}} = \{x_1, x_2, ..., x_n\}\). The induced probability function \(P_X\) is \[P_X(X = x_i) = P(\{\omega_j \in {\cal{S}} : X(\omega_j) = x_i\} \]
Distribution Functions
Every random variable has associated with it a distribution function called the cumulative distribution function (CDF).
The CDF of a random variable \(X\) is denoted by \(F_X(x)\), and is defined as \[ F_X(x) = P_X(X \le x), \text{ for all } x \in {\cal{X}} \] Right continuous function: \[\lim_{x \rightarrow x_0^+} f(x)= f(x_0)\] Properties of a CDF:

\(\lim_{x \rightarrow -\infty} F_X(x) = 0\) and \(\lim_{x \rightarrow \infty} F_X(x) = 1\)
\(F_X(x)\) is a non-decreasing function of \(x\)
\(F_X(x)\) is a right-continuous function

Continuous Random Variables
A random variable \(X\) is defined to be continuous if \(F_X(x)\) is a continuous function of \(x\).
Discrete Random Variables
A random variable \(X\) is said to be discrete if \(F_X(x)\) is a step function of \(x\).
Identically Distributed
If \(X\) and \(Y\) have CDFs \(F_X\) and \(F_Y\) and are identically distributed, then \[ F_X(x) = F_Y(x) \text{ for all } x\]

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text:
Transformations
Transformations take a set and map it onto a new sample space and return a new set.

Let \(X\) be a random variable with CDF \(F_X(x)\) and \(Y = g(X)\) be a function of \(X\).
Since \(X\) is a random variable, then \(Y = g(X)\) is a random variable. Therefore for a given set \(A\) \[ P(Y \in A) = P(g(X) \in A)\]
The distribution of \(Y\) will depend on \(g\) and on \(F_X(x)\).
\(g\) defines a mapping from \(\cal{X}\) to a new sample space, which we denote as \(\cal{Y}\)
The inverse mapping of \(g(X) = G^{-1}(Y)\): \[ g^{-1}(A) = \{x \in {\cal{X}}: g(x) \in A\} \]


Monotone Transformations
The easiest transforms are when \(g\) is monotone.
Properties of a monotone function:

One-to-one: each value \(x\) goes to only one \(y\), and each value of \(y\) comes from at most one value \(x\).
onto: For each \(y \in {\cal{Y}}\), here is an \(x \in {\cal{X}}\) such that \(g(x) = y\).

Steps for monotone transform:

Define \(\cal{X}\) and \(\cal{Y}\). \[\begin{align*}
{\cal{X}} &= \{x : f_X(x) > 0 \} \\
{\cal{Y}} &= \{y : y = g(x) \text{ for some } x \in {\cal{X}} \}
\end{align*}\]
If \(g\) is an increasing monotone function of \(\cal{X}\), \[ F_Y(y) = F_X(g^{-1}(y)) \text{ for } y \in {\cal{Y}} \]
If \(g\) is a decreasing function on \({\cal{X}}\) and \(X\) is a continuous random variable, \[ F_Y(y) = 1 - F_X\left(g^{-1}(y)\right) \text{ for } y \in \cal{Y} \]

The PDF of a monotone transform will be \[\begin{equation}
  f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y))\left|\frac{d}{dy} g^{-1}(y)\right|
\end{equation}\]


Non-monotone transformation
A non-monotone function will need to be split up into separate functions \(g_i(x)\) at the points of the partition \(A_1, A_2, .., A_k\) of \(\cal{X}\) such that \(P(X \in A_0) = 0\).

Each \(g_i(x)\) is monotone on \(A_i\)
The set \({\cal{Y}} = \{y: y = g_i(x)\}\) for some \(x \in A_i\) for each \(i = 1, 2, ..., k\)
\(g^{-1}(y)\) has a continuous derivative on \(\cal{Y}\) for rach \(i = 1, 2, ..., k\). Then \[\begin{equation}
f_Y(y) = \sum_{i=1}^k f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \left| \frac{d}{dy} g^{-1}(y) \right|
\end{equation}\]

Probability Integral Transform Let \(X\) have a continuous CDF \(F_X(x)\) and define the random variable \(Y\) as \(Y = F_X(X)\). Then \(Y\) is uniformly distributed on \((0, 1)\); that is , \(P(Y \le y) = y\) for $ 0 < y < 1$.

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text:
Expectations
The expected value of a random variable \(X\) is denoted by \(E[X]\). The expected value is a weighted average of the values of \(X\), where the weights are the assigned from the PMF or PDF.

If \(X\) is discrete with PMF \(f_X(x)\), then \[ E[X] = \sum x f_X(x) \]
If \(X\) is continuous with PDF \(f_X(x)\), then \[ E[X] = \int x f_X(x) dx \]

We can also take the expected value of transformations or functions of the data:

If \(X\) is discrete with PMF \(f_X(x)\), then \[ E[g(x)] = \sum g(x) f_X(x) \]
If \(X\) is continuous with PDF \(f_X(x)\), then \[ E[X] = \int g(x) f_X(x) dx \]

Properties of expectations:
Let \(X\) be a random variable and let \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) be real constants. Then for any functions \(g_1(x)\) and \(g_2(x)\) whose expectation exist

\(E[ag_1(X) + bg_2(X) + c] = aE[g_i(X)] + bE[g_2(X)] + c\)
If \(g_1(x) \ge 0\) for all \(x\), then \(E[g_1(x)] \ge 0\)
If \(g_1(x) \ge g_2(x)\) for all \(x\), then \(E[g_1(x)] \ge E[g_2(x)]\)
If \(a \le g_1(x) \le b\) for all \(x\), then \(a \le E[g_1(X)] \le b\)


Moments
For each integer \(n\), the \(n\)-th moment of \(X\), denoted \(\mu_n^\prime\), is defined as \[ \mu_n^\prime = E[X^n] \] The \(n\)-th central moment of \(X\), denoted \(\mu_n\), is defined as \[ \mu_n = E[(X - \mu)^n] \] Note on the two most important moments:
The two most important moments are the 1st moment and the second central moment, called the mean and variance respectively. \[\begin{align*}
  \mu &= E[X] \\
  \text{Var}(X) &= E[(X - E[X])^2] \\
  &\text{or} \\
  \text{Var}(X) &= E[X^2] - (E[X])^2
\end{align*}\]

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text:
Discrete Distributions
Expected value is defined as: \(E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X f_X(x)\)


Bernoulli(\(p\))
  PMF:\(f(x|p) = p^x(1-p)\)
  Mean: \(\mu = p\)
  Variance: \(\sigma^2 = p(1-p)\)
Binomial(\(n, p\))
  PMF:\(f(x| n, p) = \binom{n}{x} p^x {(1-p)}^{n-x}\)
  Mean: \(\mu = \text{E}[X] = np\)
  variance: \(\sigma^2 = \text{Var}[X] = np(1-p)\)
Geometric(\(p\))
  PMF: \(f(x|p) = p{(1-p)}^{x-1}\)
  CDF: \(F(X) = 1 - {(1-p)}^x\)
  Mean: \(\mu = \frac{1}{p}\)
  \(\sigma^2 = \frac{1-p}{p^2}\)

Poisson(\(\lambda\))
  PMF: \(f(x|\lambda) = \frac{\lambda^{x}e^{-\lambda}}{x!}\)
  Mean: \(\mu = \lambda\)
  \(\sigma^2 = \lambda\)
Negative Binomial(\(r, p\))
  PMF: \(f(x|r, p) = \binom{r + x + 1}{x} p^r (1-p)^x\)
  Mean: \(\mu = \frac{r(1-p)}{p}\)
  \(\sigma^2 = \frac{r(1-p)}{p^2}\)
Hypergeometric(\(N, M, K\))
  PMF: \(f(x|N, M, K) = \frac{\binom{M}{x}\binom{N-M}{K-x}}{\binom{N}{K}}\)
  Mean: \(\mu = \frac{KM}{N}\)
  \(\sigma^2 = \frac{KM}{N}\frac{(N-M)(N-K)}{N(N-1)}\)

title: Theory of Statistics notes
text:
Continuous Distributions
The expected value of a coninuous distribution is, \(\text{E}[X] = \int_{x \in {\mathcal{A}}} x f(X) dx\)


Uniform(\(a, b\))
  \(f(x|a, b) = \frac{1}{b - a}\)
  \(\mu = (a + b) / 2\)
  \(\sigma^2 = (b - a)^2 / 12\)
exponential(\(\lambda\))
  \(f(x|\lambda) = \frac{1}{\lambda} e^{- x / \lambda}\)
  \(F(x|\lambda) = 1 - e^{-x / \lambda}\)
  \(\mu = \lambda\)
  \(\sigma^2 = \lambda^2\)
Normal(\(\mu, \sigma^2\))
  \(f(x| \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma^2}} \exp\{-\frac{x - \mu}{2 \sigma^2}\}\)
  \(\mu = \mu\)
  \(\sigma^2 = \sigma^2\)

Gamma(\(\alpha, \beta\))
  \(f(x|\alpha, \beta) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)\beta^{\alpha}} x^{\alpha - 1} \exp{-x / \beta}\)
  \(\mu = \alpha \beta\)
  \(\sigma^2 = \alpha \beta^2\)
Beta(\(\alpha, \beta\))
  \(f(x| \alpha, \beta) = \frac{1}{\beta(\alpha, \beta)} x^{\alpha - 1} {(1-x)}^{\beta-1}\)
  \(\mu = \alpha / (\alpha + \beta)\)
  \(\sigma^2 = (\alpha \beta) / \{(\alpha + \beta)^2(\alpha + \beta + 1)\}\)

title: Linux for statistics
text:
With so many linux distributions available, what operating system to choose is a big task. For those that don’t know, there are over 600 different linux distributions being actively maintained. To learn more about linux distributions check here. Essentially there are 3 linux distributions that all others are based on, there are of course exceptions. It all boils down to what package manager the distributions uses and the updating structure of the system.
I’ve opted to use ubuntu or ubuntu based distributions for my systems. I have also used fedora before with no issues, both are great choices with large user bases and great comunities. Ubuntu has a LTS(long term support) release cycles that is updated once a year in april and is supported for 4 years. This means that the system will continue to get updates for 4 years.

title: Linux for statistics
text:
Post install

R and tidyverse install
Since Ubuntu doesn’t have the most up to date version of R, we need to add the key to the package manager so we can get the newest versions directly from cran.
To do this copy and past the following code into the terminal as a user with sudo access.
wget -qO- https://cloud.r-project.org/bin/linux/ubuntu/marutter_pubkey.asc | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/r-project.gpg
Next we add the R sources to the sources that apt will look through by running the following in the terminal.
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/r-project.gpg] https://cloud.r-project.org/bin/linux/ubuntu jammy-cran40/" | sudo tee -a /etc/apt/sources.list.d/r-project.list
Now that we have added cran to apt as a source we want to update the source list in apt using apt update.
sudo apt update
Then finally installing R we can simply run the following in the terminal to install the latest version of R.
sudo apt install r-base
Now the most recent version of R should be install this can be checked by running R in your terminal or by typing R --version in the terminal.


Tidyverse and dependencies
Before installing the tidyverse package in R we need to install some dependencies on the operating system. To do this run the following in the terminal.
sudo apt install r-base-dev libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev libxml2-dev libfontconfig1-dev libharfbuzz-dev libfribidi-dev libfreetype6-dev libpng-dev libtiff5-dev libjpeg-dev
After the dependencies are installed using R and install.packages() we can install the tidyverse and any other packages you need.


Additional R packages
Aside from the tidyverse some other helpful R packages are as follows.

quarto: For report and presentation creation.

title: Linux for statistics
text:
Other lanuages

Python 3 is already installed on linux, I don’t use it much, but when I need it I google how to install what I need on ubuntu. pip can be installed as follows.

# install pip
sudo apt install python3-pip

Julia can be installed in the software center if you search for it.
Latex can be installed through texlive, to do this copy the following in to bash. There are many LaTeX editors available on linux through the software center. I prefer to just use visual studio code with the LaTeX workshop extention as my TeX editor.

# install texlive full
sudo apt install texlive-full

title: Linux for statistics
text:
Software and IDEs
In terms of installing applications for coding like Rstudio can be installed from their website. Just find the download for ubuntu 22 and then it installs much like windows or mac. Open the downloaded file in the software store and click install to install the software.
For other software you can install similarly if it isn’t in the software store. Many apps are able to install right in the software center if you search its there. For example:

Visual Studio Code
Zotero
Zoom
Slack
Todoist
etc.
